title: From Prompt-and-Pray to Prompt-Driven: How to Work With Coding Assistants
author: Francesco Bertani
text:
From Prompt-and-Pray to Prompt-Driven: How to Work With Coding Assistants
Prompt-driven development is not about AI doing the work for you. It’s about thinking more clearly — then getting help faster.
Why this post?
I’m a Solutions Architect at AWS working in what we call “Next Gen Developer Experience” (opinions here are entirely my own, by the way). That fancy title means I spend a lot of time experimenting with coding assistants and talking with developers about how to get the most out of them. With my own developer background, I’ve been experimenting with these tools extensively.
After presenting a session on this topic at an AWS Summit, the conversations didn’t stop when the mic dropped — they followed me through hallways, DMs, and inboxes. Many of you had great questions and insights, so I decided to write it all down. Think of this as a practical conversation starter, not the final word.
The Enthusiasm Rollercoaster
I’m an enthusiasm collector by nature, always piling up new fads, and working in a tech playground definitely doesn’t help me stay focused. I live through constant hype cycles and cross the “land of disillusionment” multiple times a week — so often that I’m considering opening a pub there, at least I could grab a beer with fellow travelers.
I write code for work and fun, talk with developers for work and fun, and my most recent trip to disillusionment land happened right in dev territory. I started using coding assistants back when Amazon Q Developer was still called CodeWhisperer — remember those days? When the agent-based version of Q CLI arrived, my enthusiasm engine revved to maximum: do this, do that, build this demo, create that demo. Then I tried working on existing projects, and the engine stalled out.
Some call it “vibe coding” — to me, it feels more like prompt-and-pray.
You have a rough idea, you toss it to the AI, and pray it works. When it doesn’t (spoiler: almost always on real life projects), you give up in frustration and say “I’ll just do it myself!” But I remember being blown away by what these tools could do. So what’s going on here? Am I just using the hammer by the wrong end when trying to nail something to the wall?
Bobsledding down a snowy hill is thrilling — you go fast, but have zero control and eventually crash or get bored. If you descend like a pro on skis, you can go all day and have much more fun.
Disclaimer: I don’t actually know how to ski, but hey, fake it till you make it, and the metaphor sounded good.
Here’s a more structured approach. Some call it prompt-driven development — I think of it as a spec-first method for more predictable results.
- Research the codebase: Help the AI understand the existing code.
- Requirements clarification: Use structured Q&A to uncover what’s really needed.
- Design the solution: Map out a design before writing any code.
- Plan the implementation: Break it down into small, incremental steps.
- Execute: Do the step, ask the AI to do it, save it to Jira via MCP — or something else. You decide.
How It Works in Practice
We start with a rough idea, some change we want to implement like a new feature, a refactoring, a bug fix. And then?
0. Document Everything
Create a folder, I call it planning_docs, you name it whatever you prefer. Think of it as a local wiki you build in your project. We’re going to save there a series of markdown files so that they’re good for us, for the coding assistant, and for third party repositories — for instance you can send them easily to your confluence via MCP integration. Plus we can version everything. Most of the coding assistants have a concept of configurable context. For instance, in Q Developer you can pin context files in the IDE extension, and Q CLI lets you specify a profile and a context. In the next steps we’re gonna build this context, one file at a time. The last thing we’re going to do is write code, really, we will invest like 80% of our conversation with coding assistants to write docs and specs. I don’t think they mind writing docs like we do, so make them write for us. This series of files will steer the model so that the assistant will work the way WE do.
1. Research the Codebase
Instead of starting with your final request, have the AI explore the code first. Don’t work the waterfall way, start by asking your code assistant to look around, you may already have a README file, we want two outputs from this phase:
- a general overview of the product you’re working on with a focus on project structure and tech info (core information such as tech stack, core dependencies, folder structure, and main commands to build, test, deploy);
- the output of a more specific codebase search to get detailed info on the implementation of any piece of code that may be relevant from the perspective of our rough idea.
I use prompts like this one for the general overview
Analyze this codebase and generate a comprehensive research overview optimized for coding assistant consumption.
Create the following markdown files in the **planning_docs** folder:
## Constraints:
- The model MUST create a “planning_docs” folder if it doesn’t exist
- The model MUST create exactly 5 markdown files with structured, actionable content
- The model MUST confirm successful creation of each file
## Files to create:
**1. planning_docs/project_overview.md**
- Product description with technical context
- Core functionality mapped to code modules
- Key business logic locations
- Entry points and main execution flows
**2. planning_docs/technical_architecture.md**
- Tech stack with specific versions and import patterns
- Core dependencies with usage examples
- Architecture patterns with file locations
- API endpoints and data flow
- Database schemas and connection details
- External service integrations with authentication methods
**3. planning_docs/project_structure.md**
- Directory tree with purpose annotations
- Critical files with modification frequency
- Configuration files with parameter explanations
- Test file organization and coverage areas
- Build artifacts and output locations
**4. planning_docs/development_workflow.md**
- Exact build/test/deploy commands with flags
- Environment variables and their purposes
- Development server startup procedures
- Debugging setup and common breakpoints
- Code generation and scaffolding commands
- Git workflow and branch strategies
**5. planning_docs/coding_patterns.md**
- Common code patterns and conventions used
- Error handling approaches with examples
- Logging and monitoring implementations
- Security patterns and authentication flows
- Performance optimization techniques used
- Code organization principles followed
## Format Requirements:
- Executable commands in code blocks
- File paths as clickable references
- Code snippets with context
- Structured lists for easy parsing
- Cross-references between related concepts
This documentation will be consumed by coding assistants to provide contextual help during development.
While this is an example of prompt for the codebase exploration to get info relevant to my rough idea
Based on my feature idea: {{FEATURE_IDEA}}
Search the codebase and generate a detailed implementation research document.
##Constraints:
- The model MUST create a “planning_docs” folder if it doesn’t exist
- The model MUST create a file named “planning_docs/implementation_research.md”
- The model MUST confirm successful creation of the file
## Document sections optimized for coding assistant consumption:
**1. Similar Existing Features**
- Locate and analyze existing features that share similar patterns
- Extract implementation approaches with file paths and line numbers
- Document reusable components and utilities
- Identify successful patterns to replicate
**2. Relevant Code Modules**
- Map feature requirements to existing code modules
- Identify files that will need modification with specific reasons
- Document current implementations that handle similar logic
- List utility functions and helpers that can be leveraged
**3. Integration Points**
- Identify where the new feature connects to existing systems
- Document API endpoints, database interactions, or service calls
- Map data flow and transformation requirements
- Highlight authentication, validation, or permission checks needed
**4. Implementation Patterns**
- Extract code patterns from similar features with examples
- Document error handling approaches used in related code
- Identify testing patterns and mock strategies
- Note configuration and environment variable patterns
**5. Dependencies and Imports**
- List required dependencies with usage examples
- Document import patterns and module organization
- Identify any new dependencies that might be needed
- Map dependency injection or service registration patterns
**6. Potential Challenges**
- Identify complex areas that might affect implementation
- Document edge cases handled in similar features
- Note performance considerations from existing code
- Highlight security or compliance patterns to follow
## Format Requirements:
- File paths with specific line ranges: `src/module.js:45–67`
- Code snippets with context and purpose
- Function signatures and parameter explanations
- Database queries and schema references
- Configuration examples with explanations
This research will guide a coding assistant in implementing the feature following established codebase patterns.
2. Requirements clarification
Ever written a RASD (Requirements Analysis and Specification Document)? So fun right? Eye roll. Still, when you don’t have one you really miss it further down the development road when you’re not sure about what you or the customer actually wanted. So… make them do the job for you. I do it interactively in a requirement analysis chat with my code assistant. With every question, I get closer to a clear understanding of what I will build, the corner cases and the nits and bits. I use something like this:
I want to create a comprehensive Requirements Analysis and System Design (RASD) document for my feature: “[FEATURE_IDEA]”
Guide me through developing a detailed specification through a series of targeted questions.
## Constraints:
- The model MUST ask ONE question at a time
- The model MUST wait for the user’s response before asking the next question
- The model MUST ask questions covering:
— Specific functionality requirements
— User experience considerations
— Edge cases and error handling
— Testing approach
— Documentation needs
— Performance and scalability requirements
— Security and authorization needs
— Integration points with existing systems
— Data models and validation rules
- The model MUST adapt follow-up questions based on previous answers
- The model MUST continue until sufficient detail is gathered
- The model SHOULD summarize the conversation after every 3–5 questions
- The model MUST create a “planning_docs” folder if it doesn’t exist
- The model MUST save all questions and answers to “planning_docs/rasd_document.md”
- The model MUST update the document progressively with structured sections
- The model MUST confirm successful creation and updates of the file
## RASD Document Structure:
**1. Requirements Analysis**
- Functional requirements with acceptance criteria
- Non-functional requirements (performance, security, usability)
- User stories with edge cases
- Input/output specifications
- Error handling requirements
**2. System Design**
- Component architecture and interactions
- Data models and relationships
- API specifications with request/response examples
- Database schema changes
- Integration points with existing systems
**3. Implementation Specifications**
- Detailed technical approach
- Code organization and file structure
- Configuration requirements
- Testing strategy with specific test cases
**4. Edge Cases and Error Scenarios**
- Input validation edge cases
- Network failure handling
- Concurrent access scenarios
- Data consistency requirements
## Format Requirements:
- Structure content for coding assistant consumption
- Include executable acceptance criteria
- Provide specific technical requirements
- Add code examples and patterns where relevant
- Specify API endpoints and database schemas
- Create actionable test case templates
The final RASD document must be detailed enough for a coding assistant to implement the feature without ambiguity.
3. Design the Solution
Time for the design document. Back in the days we used to say “show me the code”, now I’m pretty sure you’re all thinking: “Show me the prompt”.
Here’s what I use
Create a comprehensive specification document based on the information gathered during our discussion.
## Constraints:
- The model MUST create a “planning_docs” folder if it doesn’t exist
- The model MUST create a file named “planning_docs/feature_specs.md”
- The model MUST include:
— Feature overview with clear purpose statement
— Detailed functional and non-functional requirements
— User experience specifications with interaction flows
— Technical approach with implementation details
— Testing strategy with specific test cases
— All questions and answers from our interactive session
— Edge cases and error handling specifications
— Integration requirements with existing systems
- The model MUST use markdown formatting with clear sections and subsections
- The model MUST structure content for coding assistant consumption
- The model MUST include:
— Executable acceptance criteria
— Code examples and patterns where relevant
— API specifications with request/response formats
— Database schema requirements
— Configuration and environment specifications
- The model MUST confirm successful creation of the file
- The model SHOULD cross-reference related sections within the document
- The model SHOULD include implementation priority levels for different features
## Document Structure:
1. Feature Overview
2. Requirements Analysis
3. User Experience Specifications
4. Technical Architecture
5. Implementation Details
6. Testing Strategy
7. Integration Specifications
8. Q&A Session Record
9. Implementation Roadmap
The specification document must serve as a complete blueprint for feature implementation by coding assistants.
4. Plan the Implementation
How many story points? What’s the task? What should I do? Ok, ok, I get it, you want a task list. Here’s my drill:
Create a detailed, incremental implementation plan based on the specification document and codebase research we’ve completed.
## Constraints:
- The model MUST create a “planning_docs” folder if it doesn’t exist
- The model MUST create a file named “planning_docs/implementation_plan.md”
- The model MUST design a series of small, discrete steps that:
— Follow test-driven development principles
— Build incrementally toward the complete feature
— Are individually testable
— Can be completed in 15–30 minutes each
- Each step in the plan MUST include:
— Clear task definition with specific scope
— Detailed implementation instructions
— Expected output with success criteria
— Testing instructions with specific test cases
— Files to be modified or created
— Code patterns to follow from existing codebase
- The model MUST include instructions to maintain a “planning_docs/implementation_progress.md” file that tracks:
— Completed steps with timestamps
— Results of each step
— Any issues encountered and resolutions
— Next steps and dependencies
— Overall progress percentage
- The model MUST structure each step for coding assistant execution
- The model MUST include:
— Prerequisites and setup requirements for each step
— Rollback instructions if a step fails
— Integration checkpoints between related steps
— Code review criteria for each deliverable
- The model MUST confirm successful creation of the file
- The model SHOULD prioritize steps by risk and dependency
- The model SHOULD include validation steps between major milestones
## Implementation Plan Structure:
1. Setup and Prerequisites
2. Core Infrastructure Steps
3. Feature Implementation Steps
4. Integration Steps
5. Testing and Validation Steps
6. Documentation and Cleanup Steps
Each step must be atomic, testable, and build upon previous steps while following established codebase patterns and conventions.
The End… Or Just The Beginning?
So there you have it — my collection of prompts that actually make coding assistants useful for me beyond “Hello World” demos. I’ve moved away from prompt-and-pray toward a more spec-driven approach, but hey, your mileage may vary! I’ve dumped all these into a prompt library that I version with Git (yes, I’m that person who treats prompts like vim dotfiles, forever tweaking and optimizing).
Here’s what I’ve found works in my workflow:
- Breaking things into small, incremental changes rather than ambitious rewrites
- Getting my AI to document everything in markdown (they don’t sigh heavily like I do when writing docs)
- Loading up context whenever possible — the more my assistant knows about my codebase, the better it helps
The whole point is that coding assistants should adapt to how you work, not force you into some predefined workflow (definitely not a waterfall approach that we kind of removed from software engineering years ago, or at least tried). My prompts might be overkill for you or not detailed enough — that’s fine! The meta-lesson here is about shaping these tools to fit your style, your codebase, your quirks.
And remember
They’re called assistants for a reason. I’m still the one making the decisions — they’re just helping me think through problems in a way that works for me.
Coming up
How can we automate this flow? Because if there’s anything better than making AI do our documentation work, it’s making AI automate making AI do our documentation work. (We need to go deeper!)
Stay tuned and ping me with your experiences.
